name: Kigali Genocide Memorial (Gisozi)
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: The principal national memorial and final resting place for over 250,000 victims. Three permanent exhibitions document the genocide, a children's memorial, and a comparative exhibition on global genocidal violence. Inscribed UNESCO World Heritage 2023.

testimony.text: We hid for weeks. When it was finally over and we emerged, the streets were silent. Everyone was gone. I walked through neighborhoods I had known my whole life and recognized nothing. The memorial gives them a place — a name, a grave, dignity. Without it, the world would forget them. I will not let them be forgotten.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor testimony, Kigali Genocide Memorial archives

context: Opened on the tenth anniversary of the genocide in April 2004, the memorial was established through a partnership between the Kigali City Council and the Aegis Trust. Remains were brought from across the capital — from streets, rivers, and shallow graves — and interred in mass graves. The memorial includes a Wall of Names that continues to grow as victims are identified. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2023.

name: Murambi Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: A former technical school where an estimated 45,000–65,000 Tutsi were directed under false promises of French military protection. Preserved mummified remains in the former classrooms bear witness to the scale of the atrocity. UNESCO World Heritage Site 2023.

testimony.text: They told us the French soldiers would protect us at the school. Thousands walked for hours to reach it. When we arrived, there was no water. No food. We grew weaker every day. Then on the night of the twenty-first, they came. I survived because I was buried under others. I carry them with me in every breath.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Murambi memorial records

context: The Murambi Technical School was still under construction in April 1994 when local officials directed Tutsi civilians there. After days without provisions, soldiers and Interahamwe militia attacked on the night of April 21. The memorial displays preserved remains in the former classrooms. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023.

name: Bisesero Genocide Memorial
type: Memorial of Resistance & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: Known as the 'Memorial of Resistance,' honoring the Tutsi community of Bisesero who organized armed defense of the hills for over two months before being overwhelmed. Of an original community exceeding 50,000, approximately 1,500 survived. UNESCO World Heritage Site 2023.

testimony.text: We fought with stones, with sticks, with anything we could find. Every day they came, and every day we fought. For sixty-two days we held the hills. When the French soldiers finally arrived, they said they would return to help. They came back three days later. In those three days, thousands more of us died. We were the Hill of Resistance. We are still standing.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts of Bisesero resistance, multiple survivor testimonies

context: Bisesero's hills saw the longest sustained resistance of the genocide. The Tutsi community organized themselves against militia and military forces from April through late June. French forces from Operation Turquoise made initial contact on June 27 but delayed their return, during which thousands more were killed. The memorial has nine buildings representing the nine former communes of Kibuye Province.

name: Nyamata Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: A Catholic church in Bugesera district where over 10,000 people seeking sanctuary were killed. The church preserves bloodstained clothing, personal belongings, and human remains. The iron door still bears machete scars. UNESCO World Heritage Site 2023.

testimony.text: I was a child when we ran to the church. My mother told me that God would protect us in His house. When they broke through the iron door, I hid under the bodies. I could hear everything. I lay there for two days before I moved. The clothes hanging in that church — those were worn by people I knew. By my family. This church is their voice now.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented child survivor testimony, Nyamata memorial records

context: The Nyamata Catholic Church served as a traditional place of refuge for the Tutsi community of Bugesera. When the genocide began, thousands gathered at the church. On April 14–15, 1994, militia and soldiers broke through the reinforced iron doors and massacred those inside. The memorial preserves the church as it was found. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023.

name: Ntarama Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: A former Catholic church 1 km from Nyamata, preserved exactly as it was found. The floor contains bones intermingled with clothing, shoes, and personal items. Approximately 5,000 people were killed here. UNESCO World Heritage Site 2023.

testimony.text: The church at Ntarama was small, but we believed we would be safe inside. They came with grenades first, then machetes. When you walk in today, you walk on the floor where we fell. The bones you see were people who sang hymns on Sunday. People who shared food with their neighbors. Do not look away. They deserve to be seen.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Ntarama memorial records

context: Ntarama Church, located in Bugesera district just 1 km from Nyamata, was one of the first genocide memorials established in Rwanda in 1995, with the support of the survivors' organization Ibuka. The memorial preserves the church interior as it was found after the massacre.

name: Nyarubuye Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: A former Catholic church, convent, and school where approximately 20,000–51,000 people were killed. One of the first massacre sites documented by international media. UNESCO World Heritage Site 2023.

testimony.text: Nyarubuye was supposed to be different. The parish had always been a safe place. When the killing started, people came from every direction — from the hills, from the valleys. By the time the journalists arrived weeks later and showed the world what happened here, there was no one left to tell them. I tell it now because I am one of the few who can.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented witness accounts, Nyarubuye memorial archives

context: Nyarubuye parish in Kirehe district became one of the first massacre sites documented by international media. The compound held an estimated 20,000+ people who had gathered from the surrounding hills. The attack began on April 15 and continued for several days. Remains of victims and their belongings are preserved at the site.

name: Nyanza-Kicukiro Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: Located at the former ETO school in Kicukiro. When UN peacekeepers withdrew on April 11, thousands of civilians were marched to Nyanza hill and massacred. One of the most documented failures of international intervention. UNESCO World Heritage Site 2023.

testimony.text: The soldiers — the blue helmets — they were our only hope. When they started packing their vehicles, a silence fell over all of us. Mothers stopped singing to their children. We watched them drive away. Then we were marched to the hill. On April 11th every year, I stand on that hill and I remember what it means to be abandoned by the world.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts of the ETO/Kicukiro withdrawal

context: Belgian UNAMIR peacekeepers were ordered to withdraw on April 11, abandoning approximately 4,000 civilians who had sheltered at the ETO school. Those civilians were marched to Nyanza hill and killed. The site stands as a permanent record of the international community's failure to act.

name: Camp Kigali Belgian Peacekeepers Memorial
desc: Commemorates the ten Belgian UNAMIR peacekeepers murdered at Camp Kigali on April 7, 1994, after being sent to protect Prime Minister Uwilingiyimana. Their deaths triggered Belgium's full withdrawal, leaving Rwanda without protection.

testimony.text: They came to protect us, these young soldiers from Belgium. Within a day of the president's plane being shot down, they were dead. When Belgium announced it was pulling out all its troops, we understood: we were alone. The memorial for those ten men also stands for all of us who were abandoned.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from UNAMIR records and survivor testimony
testimony.source: Genocide Archive Rwanda / Belgian Federal Government

context: On April 7, 1994, ten Belgian UNAMIR soldiers escorting Prime Minister Agathe Uwilingiyimana were captured at Camp Kigali, tortured, and killed by Rwandan Presidential Guard soldiers. The murders prompted Belgium to withdraw all its peacekeeping troops, significantly reducing UNAMIR's capacity to protect civilians.

name: Cyanika Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Burera district on the Uganda border, Cyanika memorial holds the remains of approximately 35,000 victims from the northern highlands. Victims included people who tried to flee toward Uganda.

testimony.text: We tried to cross into Uganda. The border was so close. But they had checkpoints on every road, and people who knew us pointed us out. At Cyanika, the hills that could have been an escape became a death trap. The memorial stands at the border between life and what we lost.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Northern Province survivors

context: Cyanika, in Burera district near the Ugandan border, was a site where thousands of Tutsi attempted to flee the genocide northward. Many were intercepted at roadblocks. The memorial holds approximately 35,000 victims and serves as one of the major commemorative sites of the Northern Province.

name: Gahanga Memorial
desc: Located in Kicukiro district south of Kigali, Gahanga memorial commemorates thousands of victims killed in this agricultural corridor. Victims were targeted at roadblocks on roads leading south from the capital.

testimony.text: Gahanga was on the road they used to flee the city. But there was no fleeing. Every few hundred meters, there was a barrier. Men with lists. I crossed because I had papers they didn't question. Most did not cross.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Kicukiro district, 1994

context: Gahanga, south of Kigali in Kicukiro district, saw intense violence as Tutsi civilians attempted to flee the capital. Roadblocks controlled by Interahamwe militia and security forces intercepted families attempting to escape southward.

name: Kabgayi Genocide Memorial
desc: Located at the historic Catholic diocese of Kabgayi in Muhanga district, one of Rwanda's oldest and largest Catholic institutions. Three bishops and hundreds of clergy and refugees were killed here. A major center of the Catholic Church's painful history during the genocide.

testimony.text: Kabgayi was the heart of the Catholic Church in Rwanda. When the bishops were killed — men of God — we understood that no title, no vocation, no faith protected you. The killers did not see a bishop. They saw a Tutsi. The memorial asks us never to look away from that fact.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Kabgayi diocese archives
testimony.source: Genocide Archive Rwanda / Catholic Church Rwanda

context: The diocese of Kabgayi, established in 1900, was Rwanda's oldest Catholic see. During the genocide, refugees sheltered at the seminary and churches, but militia killed thousands within the compound. Three bishops — Vincent Nsengiyumva (Kigali), Thaddée Nsengiyumva (Kabgayi), and Joseph Ruzindana (Byumba) — were murdered by RPF soldiers on June 5, 1994, in a separate incident.

name: Kabuye Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Gasabo district northeast of central Kigali, Kabuye memorial commemorates thousands of victims from this suburban area. Killings here began within hours of April 6 as Interahamwe established control of the district.

testimony.text: Kabuye was a neighborhood of teachers, nurses, civil servants. Within twenty-four hours of the president's death, the lists were out and the killing began. I knew every name on those lists. I had taught their children.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Gasabo district survivors

context: Kabuye, in Gasabo district, was one of the Kigali suburban areas where organized killings began on April 7, 1994. The Interahamwe worked from pre-prepared lists of Tutsi residents. The memorial commemorates victims from the sector and surrounding neighborhoods.

name: Kaduha Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Gisagara district, Southern Province, the Kaduha memorial holds the remains of 47,311 identified victims. A church was the site of a major massacre where thousands gathered seeking sanctuary and were attacked.

testimony.text: The church at Kaduha was full — every pew, every aisle, every corner. People had walked for days from surrounding sectors. When the attack came, there was no room to move, no room to run. The number 47,311 — I want you to understand that each of those is a person who woke up that morning believing they might survive.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Gisagara district records

context: Kaduha Parish in Gisagara district was a gathering point for Tutsi civilians from across the southern plateau. The attack was coordinated with local administrative authorities. The memorial holds 47,311 remains and is one of the largest memorial sites in southern Rwanda.

name: Kamonyi Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Kamonyi district on the southern rim of Kigali, this memorial commemorates thousands of victims killed as the genocide spread from the capital outward into the surrounding districts.

testimony.text: Kamonyi was where Kigali ended and the hills began. People from the city tried to reach their family in the countryside. The countryside was not safe either. The memorial sits at that crossroads — between the city's chaos and the hills' false promise of escape.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Kamonyi district survivors

context: Kamonyi district sits between Kigali and the central plateau. As violence spread from the capital, Tutsi civilians caught in this corridor were targeted at roadblocks and during house-to-house searches. The interim government relocated to Gitarama on April 12, passing through this area.

name: Kibeho Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Nyaruguru district, Kibeho — known as a Marian apparition site — became a site of genocide and later of a post-genocide massacre of displaced persons. The memorial holds remains of 28,937 identified victims.

testimony.text: Kibeho is a place where the Virgin Mary was said to have appeared and warned of a river of blood. When the killing came, people remembered those words. They came to the shrine believing it was sacred ground. It was not protected. Nothing was protected. The memorial stands where the apparition once promised peace.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Nyaruguru district records

context: Kibeho, in Nyaruguru district, gained international attention in the 1980s as a site of alleged Marian apparitions. During the genocide, thousands sought refuge at the shrine, many of whom were killed. In April 1995, a massacre of Hutu internally displaced persons at the same site by Rwandan Patriotic Army soldiers drew further international attention.

name: Kibirizi Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Nyabihu district in the northern highlands near the Virunga volcanoes, Kibirizi memorial commemorates victims from the highland sectors where killings were organized under military coordination.

testimony.text: The highlands near the volcanoes felt like the end of the world. Remote, cold, hard to reach. We thought that distance would protect us. But the lists had been prepared months before. The distance only meant we heard what was coming a few days before it arrived.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Nyabihu district survivors

context: Kibirizi, in Nyabihu district's highland zone, saw killings organized by local officials and Interahamwe who had received weapons and training in advance. The remote terrain provided brief warning but ultimately no protection for the Tutsi communities there.

name: Kibuye Genocide Memorial
desc: Near Lake Kivu, honoring the approximately 11,000 victims killed at Gatwaro Stadium and surrounding sites. The prefect directed Tutsi civilians to gather under false promises of protection before the massacre.

testimony.text: Kibuye was beautiful. The lake, the hills, the churches. They used our trust in these places against us. When the prefect told us to gather at the stadium for our own safety, we believed him. We had always believed the authorities. That belief killed us.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor testimony, Kibuye region

context: Kibuye prefecture experienced among the highest proportional death tolls during the genocide. Prefect Clément Kayishema directed Tutsi civilians to gather at Gatwaro Stadium and at churches under false promises of protection, then facilitated the massacres.

name: Kinazi Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Ruhango district, Kinazi memorial is one of the largest in Rwanda, holding remains of approximately 60,000 victims from across the central plateau. A major site of commemoration for the southern provinces.

testimony.text: The killing in the south was methodical. It was organized. It moved from commune to commune like a schedule. In Kinazi, families who had hidden for weeks were eventually found. There was nowhere that remained safe long enough. The memorial holds 60,000. That number is not abstract to me. Each one has a face.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Ruhango district records

context: Kinazi, in Ruhango district on the central plateau, saw systematic killings organized through local administrative structures. The memorial serves as a major regional site for Southern Province, collecting remains from across multiple former communes.

name: Kiziguro Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Gatsibo district in the Eastern Province, Kiziguro memorial holds identified remains of over 20,000 victims. Violence in this area was intertwined with the RPF military advance from Uganda.

testimony.text: In Kiziguro, the genocide happened against the backdrop of war. The RPF was advancing from the east, and the killings accelerated — as if they were trying to eliminate all witnesses before anyone arrived. We were killed faster precisely because help was coming. I cannot explain that to you. I lived it.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts, Gatsibo district survivors

context: Kiziguro, in Gatsibo district, was within the zone of combat between the RPF advancing from Uganda and government forces. Killings of Tutsi civilians here were accelerated as the military situation deteriorated for the genocidal government. The memorial holds over 20,000 identified remains.

name: Mugombwa Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Gisagara district in southern Rwanda, Mugombwa memorial holds approximately 26,700 victims. Violence in this area was among the most intense in the Southern Province.

testimony.text: In Mugombwa, the hills that had fed us became the hills that hid us. But they searched every hill. Every valley. Every home. The killers knew the terrain as well as we did. Better, perhaps — they had time to prepare. The memorial stands where we could not.
testimony.attribution: Based on community memorial records, Gisagara district

context: Mugombwa, in the southernmost reaches of Rwanda near the Burundi border, was part of the former Butare prefecture where killings intensified after the replacement of the moderate Tutsi prefect Jean-Baptiste Habyarimana on April 19.

name: Mutete Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Eastern Province, Mutete memorial commemorates thousands of victims from this district. Killings were organized through local administrative structures as the genocide spread from Kigali outward.

testimony.text: Mutete was far enough from Kigali that we hoped. We sat by the radio and we hoped. But the orders came through the local administrators — the same men who had managed the roads, the schools, the markets. They used that authority to organize our deaths.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts, Eastern Province memorial records

context: The Mutete memorial commemorates victims from the Eastern Province who were killed as the genocide's organized structure extended through the national administrative network from Kigali to every corner of the country.

name: Ngororero Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Ngororero district, Western Province, this memorial commemorates 8,402 identified victims from the district. Violence was organized by local officials who coordinated with Interahamwe militia.

testimony.text: Ngororero was in the hills between Kigali and Kibuye. We were in between two killing zones, and neither direction offered safety. When the local officials gave the order, they knew exactly who to target. They had lived alongside us for years. That is what betrayal means at its deepest.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts, Ngororero district survivors

context: Ngororero district, in Western Province, was one of the areas where the genocide was organized through pre-existing local administrative structures. Interahamwe militia worked alongside local officials who had previously catalogued their Tutsi neighbors.

name: Nyamasheke Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Nyamasheke district in the Western Province on the Congo border, this memorial commemorates thousands of victims. Some victims were thrown into the Rusizi River or Lake Kivu as killers attempted to destroy evidence.

testimony.text: The lake was visible from where the killing happened. They threw bodies into the water. They believed the water would take the evidence to another country, make it disappear. The lake did not disappear the people. We built the memorial so the people could not disappear.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Nyamasheke district survivors

context: Nyamasheke, near the DRC border in Western Province, saw systematic killings with bodies disposed of in Lake Kivu and the Rusizi River. The area was later partially covered by France's Operation Turquoise humanitarian zone established in late June 1994.

name: Nyange Genocide Memorial (Kivumu Church)
desc: Located in Ngororero district, Nyange Church is where Father Athanase Seromba ordered a bulldozer to demolish the church onto approximately 2,000 people sheltering inside. One of the most documented atrocities involving a Catholic priest.

testimony.text: At Nyange, we had no weapons. We had only each other and our faith. When they demolished the walls onto us, we held hands. I felt the weight of the building and the weight of every person I had ever known. Some of us were pulled out. Most were not. The priest who gave the order was later convicted of genocide.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Nyange survivors and ICTR records

context: Nyange Church, in Ngororero district, became internationally known through the ICTR trial of Father Athanase Seromba, convicted of genocide and crimes against humanity for ordering the demolition of the church. He was sentenced to life imprisonment. The memorial marks the site of the demolished church.

name: Nyanza Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Nyanza district, Southern Province, this memorial commemorates thousands of victims from the hillside communities of this former royal capital of Rwanda. Tutsi civilians were hunted in the hills surrounding the historic town.

testimony.text: Nyanza was once the seat of the Rwandan kings. The hills around it held the graves of our ancestors. We ran to those hills to hide. But the killers knew those hills too. History could not protect us, but it could remember us. The memorial remembers us.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Nyanza district survivors

context: Nyanza, the historic royal capital of the Rwandan kingdom in Southern Province, saw its Tutsi population targeted systematically. The violence intensified after April 19 when the moderate prefect of Butare (which included this area) was removed and killed.

name: Nyundo Genocide Memorial
desc: Located at the historic Nyundo Cathedral in Rubavu district near Gisenyi, this memorial commemorates victims killed at the cathedral and seminary. Nyundo was the major Catholic episcopal seat of northwestern Rwanda.

testimony.text: Nyundo Cathedral had trained priests and musicians for generations. When people ran to the cathedral for sanctuary, it was because it had always been a place of God. But there was no sanctuary in 1994. The cathedral itself was violated. I think about the choirboys who trained there. About the organist whose name I still remember.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Rubavu district survivors

context: Nyundo, home to one of Rwanda's oldest Catholic dioceses, sits on a hill above Lake Kivu near the Congolese border. The diocese had trained clergy across the region. During the genocide, killings at the cathedral and seminary added to the pattern of religious sites violated during the 100 days.

name: Ruhanga Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Gasabo district east of Kigali, Ruhanga memorial holds approximately 37,000 victims. This site commemorates the massive killings in the eastern suburbs and hillsides as people tried to flee the capital.

testimony.text: Ruhanga was east of Kigali, where many thought they could reach the countryside and disappear. But the network of Interahamwe covered every hill. The memorial here holds 37,000. I walk through it and think: these people were trying to live. They were moving toward life. They were stopped.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Gasabo district, eastern Kigali

context: Ruhanga, in the eastern hills of Gasabo district, was a location where tens of thousands of Tutsi trying to escape Kigali were intercepted and killed. The memorial is one of the larger sites in the Kigali region and holds remains transferred from across the district.

name: Rukumberi Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in Ngoma district, Eastern Province, Rukumberi memorial holds the remains of approximately 42,000 victims. The Kagera River flows nearby — bodies thrown into the river were carried downstream to Lake Victoria and seen internationally.

testimony.text: The Kagera River was where the world finally saw us. Reporters photographed the bodies floating downstream into Uganda and Tanzania. Journalists showed those images. And the world watched, and did not come. At Rukumberi, we remember not only those who died here, but that the world saw and chose to look away.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from Ngoma district and international press records

context: Rukumberi, in Ngoma district near the Tanzanian border, was adjacent to the Kagera River corridor. Bodies thrown into the Kagera were carried into Uganda and Tanzania and photographed by international media — some of the earliest visual evidence of the genocide seen internationally. Despite this, international intervention was not forthcoming.
